narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as a tough one—76% chance of not passing, based on patterns from thousands of students like you. Picture it like this: you're starting your law school journey full-time with a solid target in sight, but the model zeroed in first on your Year 3 decile of 1.0, the lowest rung in your class, which screamed the loudest signal of struggle ahead, more than anything else in your profile. That low Year 1 decile of 1.0 piled on right behind it, painting a picture of early and persistent academic hurdles that often snowball into bar exam pitfalls. Your LSAT score of 25 then stepped in as the next big red flag, well below the levels that typically light the way to success for others. Layered with your lower-middle family income, which the model links to extra barriers in affording the prep and support needed, and your Hispanic background, where stats show steeper odds stacked against passing, it all started tipping the scales heavily toward failure. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, being male, and going full-time didn't pull things back—they nudged the risk higher in the patterns we've seen. It's not about your worth or potential; it's this specific combo that echoes stories of students who, despite grit, hit walls on exam day. The good news? You can rewrite this script—beef up those study habits, seek targeted tutoring, or retake the LSAT to shift the odds. You've got the fire; now channel it where it counts most. We're rooting for your comeback.
